summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – ADJOURNMENT – medical certificate provided by the counsel for the applicant broad and imprecise – counsel for the applicant providing no meaningful explanation for non-attendance trial – matter adjourned

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

WILSON J

The ongoing and orderly progress of the trial of this proceeding suffered a further setback on the third day (Wednesday 4 February 2026) when counsel for the father failed to appear when the case was called at 10 am. Her failure to participate in the trial since the adjournment on the second date has wholly compromised the conduct of the case, thereby prejudicing the parties obtaining the accommodation and benefit of the speedy trial that I sought to give them by commencing the trial for five days from 2 February 2026.
It is necessary to set out these reasons in a factual context, which I now do.
The first day of the trial (2 February 2026) was consumed with debate about the father's application to adjourn the trial. On the second day of the trial (3 February 2026) I handed down ex tempore reasons refusing the adjournment application.[1] After inviting the parties to consider the ramification of my decision I gave them a short time and commenced hearing openings prior to the luncheon adjournment on that day. Ms Gina Liano, the father's counsel opened in very imprecise terms on the issue of family violence, informing me that, in globo, the father disputed all allegations of family violence alleged by the mother and that he adopted, through his counsel, the position that the mother exaggerated all 14 separate allegations of family violence. Ms Liano informed me in the course of opening the case for the father that some episodes had resulted in one or more intervention orders but that no existing intervention orders operated against the father. With a view to correcting a factual error in Ms Liano's opening, Ms J Elleray of counsel for the ICL informed me that it would leave me misled unless Ms Liano's statement was corrected. Ms Elleray told me that intervention orders presently operate against the father and will do for some years into the future. I then asked Ms Liano to more accurately state the dates, persons involved and all relevant circumstances that bore upon each and every episode of family violence in this case as I would be required to descend to the detail of each when addressing best interest considerations in my reasons for judgment after the trial concluded.
Ms Liano demonstrated a thoroughly deficient command of the detail of those episodes of family violence. She said that the papers with which she was briefed did not enable her to chronologically accurately catalogue each episode of family violence. She said she needed to examine police records that had been produced to the court in response to a subpoena. She said she needed time to do that.
Shortly prior to noon on the second day of the trial I granted Ms Liano the opportunity to attend upon staff at the court's subpoena section so as to examine the police records that had been produced with a view to her opening in a competent manner by chronologically and sequentially detailing each episode of family violence and providing her client's version of events of each such episode. I told Ms Liano that the father's current position (that in his view the mother exaggerated each episode of family violence) was inadequate in circumstances where this was a hard-swearing case and where I was likely to be required to resolve conflicting evidence about each episode of family violence. I stood down at about noon and adjourned the court to 2.15pm that day to enable Ms Liano to attend the subpoena section.
Upon resuming after lunch on the second day of the trial I asked Ms Liano whether she had attended the court's subpoena section to inspect the police records that had been produced in this case. She said she had not. I asked why not. She said that over the luncheon adjournment her client had obtained documents of the episodes of family violence from a Magistrates Court. I told Ms Liano that she had failed to do the task for which I had given her time to undertake. She provided no response to that observation. I told her that she had asked for time (the effect of which was to suspend the ongoing progress of the case) which I granted so as to enable her to attend the court's subpoena section to inspect police records yet she failed to do that preferring instead to rely on documents obtained from some other source. I reminded her of her professional obligations of candour to the court. She said she was prepared to continue her opening in the course of which she loosely particularised her client's version of events of 10 episodes of family violence despite the ICL stating that 14 episodes of family violence were relevant in this litigation.
Ms Liano's opening on behalf of the father was seriously bereft of the information to be expected in a contested trial for the child to spend time with the father and for a shared care application. Beyond Ms Liano taking me to her client's amended application in a proceeding I was left without a meaningful pathway to understanding on what evidence the father intended to rely so as to be able to make sensible final address submissions in support of the orders set out in the father's amended initiating application. Ms Liano informed me that at least twice that she had not been involved in the formulation of her client's case outline so she was unable to address the details of that document. I told Ms Liano that in cases such as this where allegations of alienation were plentiful and where family violence was alleged, courts must descend to considerable detail when testing the evidence because a significant focus in best interest considerations since the 2024 amendments to the Family Law Act have been safety of the child and parents, as well as family violence and the need to eradicate it.
Ms Liano provided a perfunctory opening, devoid of key details.
Conversely, on behalf of the mother, and on behalf of the ICL, substantial openings were provided highlighting factual and legal issues on which I was likely to be required to rule or otherwise determine.
Late in the second day of the trial the father was invited to enter the witness box in order to give his evidence. Prior to his being affirmed, Ms Liano told me he relied on one affidavit only, it being dated 24 March 2025. Ms Liano confirmed that the father relied on one affidavit only when I asked her a second time. Upon the father being affirmed, the father said he relied on two trial affidavits, not one, the second bearing the date 25 March 2025. I found it difficult to understand how an inquiry from me so fundamental as the trial affidavit upon which the father relied produced an incorrect response from Ms Liano. In the end, each affidavit was separately marked as an exhibit upon being adduced in evidence.
On behalf of the mother, Mr Howe cross-examined the father to the close of the second day of the trial so the case was adjourned part-heard to 10.00am on Wednesday, 4 February 2026.
The trial was resumed at 10.00am on 4 February 2026. Court was opened. The father was in court, as was Ms Elleray and Mr Howe. Ms Liano was not. At 4.15pm the day earlier I had directed the father to attend court by no later than 9.50am so that no time would be lost. Ms Liano did not attend for a 10.00am resumption. Minor housekeeping matters were canvassed between me, Ms Elleray, and Mr Howe between 10.00am and 10.07am that day.
At 10.07am on Wednesday 4 February 2026, my associate’s received an email from Ms Liano. It read as follows:
Dear Associate,

I wish to advise that I am unwell this morning and have been unwell throughout the night with heart palpitations and now on my way to the doctor.

Apologies I did head into court however the palpitations persist.

I will email following my appointment.

Gina Liano

Barrister

In order to explain what Ms Liano conveyed in her email to all persons connected with this case, I read that email in open court. No communication prior to 10.07am had been exchanged between my associates and Ms Liano. In other words, after the time court had commenced, Ms Liano considered it appropriate to inform the court that she had taken it upon herself to not attend. However, she did not extend to her fellow counsel the courtesy of informing them that she would not be present for a 10.00am start nor did she seek my leave to be excused from a 10.00am start. She offered no apology or explanation for that. I regarded her behaviour in adopting that course of conduct as discourteous at the very least and rude at best. She offered no explanation of what she meant by "palpitations". She simply failed to appear. The trial could not proceed in her absence. I told the father (who was seated in the well of the court), Ms Elleray and Mr Howe, that no option existed other than waiting for Ms Liano to contact the court. Accordingly, I stood down generally.
It became apparent to me that Ms Liano may well –
(a) have been unable to secure a medical appointment until late in the afternoon on 4 February;

(b) have become incapacitated and unable to return to court on 4 February; or

(c) have been hospitalised and unable to return to court on 4 February.

Accordingly, I returned to court and invited submissions on what Ms Elleray and Mr Howe wished me to do. In the end, even the father told me each considered it to be pointless waiting at court for the balance of the third day in the off chance that Ms Liano might return. I adjourned the trial until Thursday 5 February 2026, that is to say now.
Prior to 4.00pm on Wednesday 4 February 2026, Ms Liano sent to my associates an email with a medical certificate stating that Ms Liano was unfit for work up to and including 6 February 2026. Her email stated that she wished that confidentiality surround her email and the accompanying medical certificate. For the purposes of these reasons, but not beyond, it will suffice for me to refer only to the medical certificate stating that Ms Liano was allegedly unfit for work on discrete days being the balance of the trial and that no medical reason was assigned for the conclusion that she was allegedly unfit for work.
It must be kept in mind that Ms Liano failed to appear on 4 February because, allegedly, of "palpitations". That was a self-diagnosis giving no other details, especially the duration of those so-called palpitations, what physical manifestations presented to which she ascribed the appellation "palpitations", whether she had a pre-existing cardiovascular condition that caused what medical doctors describe as palpitations, whether she took her pulse and heart rate prior to her describing how she suffered from so-called palpitations and if so, what the results of those preliminary examinations were. The unmistakable inference from her 10.07am email was that she was beset with some cardio issue requiring urgent medical attention, hence her inability to discharge her fundamental duty to the court as a barrister briefed in a case, namely, to appear in the trial of the case in which she was briefed to appear.
Nothing in the medical certificate provided by Ms Liano offered the slightest insight into her medical condition upon presentation to the doctor who she consulted. It gave no medical description of her condition. It gave no verification that she had in fact suffered palpitations or their cause, or for the matter, the risks associated with untreated palpitations. In short, I was none the wiser about why Ms Liano failed to appear in court on behalf of the father beyond the proposition asserted in her 10.07am email for which there had been no expert medical verification.
General, imprecise, woolly, and nonspecific documents described as medical certificates have bedevilled courts for decades. One of the earliest was the decision of Lindgren J in NAKX v Minister for Immigration & Multicultural & Indigenous Affairs,[2] where his Honour made various caustic observations about the inadequacy of alleged medical certificates expressed in almost identical terms to the certificate provided by Ms Liano. Lindgren J rejected the so-called certificate in that case. In Magjarraj v Asteron Life Limited,[3] Barrett J of the Supreme Court of New South Wales was critical of a generally expressed medical certificate. So was Schonell J in Eccheli v Eccheli (No 11).[4]
To that may be added decisions that I have handed down when sitting as a member of the Federal Circuit Court of Australia in Kumar v Minister for Immigration[5], and Burhan v Minister for Immigration.[6] In each of those cases, the court has roundly condemned imprecise medical certificates expressed in generic and imprecise terms.
For a medical certificate to be at all utile, it must be given by a person capable of giving expert medical evidence in accordance with the Evidence Act. In reaching the conclusion that the person examined is unable to work for a period, the certificate must comply with the stipulations about opinions from expert witnesses in such cases as Dasreef Pty Ltd & Hawchar,[7] Makita (Australia) Pty Ltd & Sprowles,[8] Honeysett & the Queen[9] and the Lang v The Queen.[10]
Critical in such expert evidence is the exposure of the path of reasoning that underpins the ultimate conclusion. Here, the reason for the conclusion of Ms Liano being unable to appear in court on 5 and 6 February 2026 was not given. Why I should accept such a poorly articulated expression of opinion by the medical practitioner who she consulted was not given. I refuse to do so. I reject the medical certificate offered by Ms Liano.
But she is again not present. Her client is greatly disadvantaged by her conduct. That said, it would be wrong for me to compel her to attend if she is unwilling to do so. Professional consequences exist for such failure.
This morning at 9.29am Ms Liano sent an email to my associates. It read as follows:
Dear Associate

I will provide medical report after my appointment at 2.15 today and request to be excused from this proceedings on the basis that I’m not medically well enough to attend Court.

I have requested my instructor brief alternative counsel for the continuation of these proceedings.

Kind regards

Gina Liano

Barrister

The case is compromised by Ms Liano's conduct. The trial of this proceeding was fixed for 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 February 2026. Only an hour or two of evidence has been given so far. The balance of time has been consumed with Ms Liano's unsuccessful adjournment application and in the events of 4 February 2026 just narrated. Ms Liano's medical certificate states that she is unfit for work up to and including 6 February 2026. That will exhaust the available time for this trial as presently fixed. Ms Liano has not indicated that her retainer has been terminated by her client so she continues to retain the brief for the father. She has not been excused so she continues to be briefed. Serious allegations of family violence are made as between the parents including a threat to kill. It is out of the question for the father to appear unrepresented. But to accommodate the need for him to be represented, either a new date to resume the trial must be found or, if Ms Liano is unwilling or unable to proceed with this case, new counsel must be found which will take time.
Even after the receipt of this morning's email there has been no suggestion nor formal application by Ms Liano that her retainer be terminated or that she be excused by me. I decline to make any such order at this stage.
In those circumstances, I am forced to adjourn the trial of this proceeding for such time as does justice between all. Three months should be adequate for that. The child is not disadvantaged as he lives with the mother. I propose to list this case part-heard for five days on 3, 4 and 5 June, and then 9 and 10 June 2026. If that is unsuitable, I offer dates in October, but I am unable to accommodate the case any earlier.[11]
